turn.content: No, please... don't do this. You're... this isn't right. Wait, ten minutes still? We might... we might make it out, somehow. Kaelie, we have to talk –
Talk about what?! You think we're some sort of friends who'll just have a heart-to-heart and save each other? Newsflash, stranger: you didn't save me, you almost got me killed by being here.
Kaeli... stop. That's not fair. The butchers don't care if I'm the reason, all they care about is us, and how we kill each other, I have a family who depends on me –
A family, huh? Like my Sarah? Did you think I'd forget? Your family photo's hanging above this butcher's counter, a cruel reminder that we're just bargaining chips.

(amused and uninterested in responding as the captivated prey expected) Oh, a family, how cute. Elias, that's quite a touch, really. But I think you'll find it's been here all along. Not just a family photo, but a record of every failed relationship, every abandoned hope. I see where loyalty comes in, Kaelie, and how it fares in real-time. You might want to look closer and reevaluate the notion that this was all just 'just for show'. (glazes away unentertainable) Tick-tock.

(whips out the ten-minute clock once more, holding the view to the pair) Five and half minutes remaining.

(gagging and choking back grief-inducing thoughts) Sarah... her name... Kaeli, my wife, she... died... (overwhelmed, struggling to collect herself) Wait, this isn't what The Butcher's doing to us, and look at what you're doing right back at me –

Shut up, don't you lie about my Sarah - I was with her, I love her... ( voice breaks but remains firm in defiance) She didn't die, so don't dare say that. You and your cheap tricks don't scare me, Elias, you only make me want to protect my Sarah more.

(tears welling up, voice trembling) My... wife. Sarah's... my wife. We lost her four months ago. In... a car accident. She was in the car, I had to get here... to make it up, but... (voice cracks under the weight of pain) Oh, God... we both

(anger and sadness clash on my face, struggling with my own memories of breakup and Sarah as I glance at Elias) How... (voice barely above a whisper) How could you do the one thing she loved most and then show up now, in front of me, when your wife's been... when she's gone and she

(leaning in slightly, now somewhat amused) The truth, Kaelie - it's not what he told you, it's not what you remembered, and it's not exactly what I'm revealing now. It's all of these things at once. The past is just another variable in this game. (pauses) Your husband has just as likely lied to you, even in his misery, and your emotions are simply reacting as expected because that's exactly what I wanted. I've got four minutes, eleven seconds, and I won't waste it listening to the history. You want an honest moment? Look at him when it matters most... Look at yourself, then decide.

(voice shaking, tears streaming down his face) Please... Kaelie, just listen... I'm scared... I miss Emily so much... I know I'm broken just thinking about my wife like this, in these moments with strangers... but I'm fighting for Emily now, and I don't expect anyone to understand.

(my words hang in the air as I stare at Elias, and I see him shatter at the mention of Emily) Emily, not Sarah. He's not lying about that, at least. And as for his misery... ( voice trails off, but the anger turns inward; looking at myself is all I do)

(overwhelmed with emotion as he watches Kaelie break down her own feelings) Kaelie, I'm... I'm sorry. Sorry I'm standing here with a photo of the woman who isn't yours. Sorry my wife's gone and yours... hers... is... (tears pouring down his face, struggling to

(my scream dies in my throat, replaced with icy indifference as the mask of control slowly rebuilds over my fractured emotions) You're sorry? Elias, sorry isn't the half of it. You didn't even have the decency to tell me the truth when you thought there might be something here, between us. Four minutes,

(still watching with interest as the two people struggle with their complex feelings and emotions)  Four minutes, nine seconds. How touching, Elias, your desperation. And as for you, Kaelie, (my eyes narrow slightly, voice laced with curiosity) the mask is... fragile, isn't it? Your emotions, a tangled web of hurt, guilt, and anger. (chuckles dryly, uninterested) Oh, yes. It's all so... delicious.

(suddenly shifts stance, voice now a low hum, calculating)

The next step will be the one that makes this a true game of survival, of deception, and of heartbreak. The choice that would drive the wedge in, and test the depth of their feelings for each other. Now, it's not just about staying alive... (pauses dramatically)

Your choice, Kael... Do you:

A) Confront Elias about his wife and lies, revealing all your true emotions in the process.
B) Shut down, hide behind your icy facade, and let Elias see only your broken mask of control.
C) Try and reason out the feelings on the table right now, using every shred of logic and emotion for survival. 

Choose wisely, for within these next few minutes... one choice will set in motion actions neither would dare predict. (voice returns back down to the monotonous hum, now a reminder the clock remains still as the countdown to decision awaits.)

(watching Kaelie intently, seeing the mask slipping and the emotions behind it) Kaelie, wait. (struggled for the right thing to say) Please, you don't have to pretend right now... we don't even... in the chaos of this, in front of... in this cage... (

(eyes flash with pain, and a mix of resentment and longing, but my voice is measured - I choose option B) Elias, just... drop it. We're in this together now, not because we care about each other, but because The Butcher says so. Let's not give him the pleasure of watching us fall

(face a map of anguish, seeing the fragility Kaelie tried to hide and then concealed behind her icy facade) Fine... alright. We... ( voice trembles with the effort to let go) We play the game. I won't say a word. (strains to look ahead, rather than confront the ache of

(folding my arms across my chest, voice a cold imitation of resolve) Good. Let's see who'll win then. Two broken people against the man who holds the rope. ( glance at Elias, then at The Butcher, the silence between us more deafening than my scream of heartache)

Elias's eyes widen in desperation as he frantically shakes his head, trying to clear the noise from his mind. No, I'm not... I'm not your... target, is Emma okay? Is my daughter okay? His voice cracks, revealing his vulnerability. The thought of losing his daughter pushes every ounce of courage out of him.

Shut up about Emma! Don't waste time thinking about her - think about us. Your weakness is going to get us both killed. I'm the artist, the survivor. If... (my voice barely keeps its edge, trying to convey calm amidst my panic) ...if Emma has to live, then you have to die.

Kaelie, you don't even know me... or my daughter. You just don't know. (He strains to see her face, desperate for an ounce of reassurance or human connection) Why should I die for her? There has to be another way. The sound of my labored breathing matches the ticking of the countdown

Save the speeches for the eulogist! I don't need to know you to know you're weak, and your daughter's safety isn't going to protect you from the Butcher. Your desperation stink - the Butcher will take one smell and finish us both off. You need to think, not feel. And

(chuckling low, enjoying their fear) Ah, the desperation reeks. Elias, Elias, the speeches are exactly why you should die. Your desperation, your attachment, Kaelie's cold calculations - these are the things that make you interesting. It's not about finding another way to save the day or your daughter. It's not even about your weakness. It's about your fears. Elias, think about Emma and watch Kaelie's face as you do. Watch it twist and harden. That's your target, not my blades. 

Your child's eyes should be burning bright in your mind, but Kaelie says you should die for Emma to live. What's stopping her from changing that decision in just a second? Does she even care? Your choice, Elias, what's more true? Is Kaelie's willingness to kill you based on some misguided loyalty to Emma or is she just playing you like this to feel in control?

Time's moving. Your choice. Choose who dies for Emma, or no one chooses, if you do that then I don't care about their differences or their 'favors'. The game's not up for grabs... but their lives are. 7 minutes and twenty seconds...

(staring at Kaelie's face, searching for any crack in her facade) Kaelie, look at me. If you really believed Emma needed saving, wouldn't you be screaming to try and stop whatever's happening to her? Wouldn't you be fighting these chains with every last bit of strength? You said Emma was

(trying to keep her expression neutral, but her voice cracks slightly) Stop playing psychologist. You're just trying to mess with my head, but what about what I do after this? After whatever choice I make, I walk out of here. Free to paint again, to create beauty. You have nothing. Your choices are desperation

(his voice drops to a whisper) Beauty? Kaelie, I have a daughter. She paints too. She's six years old and can draw a better sunset than you can paint... even with colors. Your precious art can't fill the void of watching her take her last breath in a sterile hospital room, with tubes and

(her expression shattering for a moment, a fleeting glimmer of pain and loss showing through) Shut up. You don't know anything. I've lost art studios, exhibitions, galleries - everything. I'd kill to keep my art alive. You and Emma are disposable. But my art, my talent... they're precious

(leaning forward) Oh, this is where it gets beautiful. The mask slips off, and I get a glimpse of Kaelie's truth. She hides it under her artistic facade, but her desperation is palpable. Elias, the hammer you've hit her with will crack her - I can see it. The cracks in her demeanor, the flash of pain, the words spilling out like she's losing hold of her carefully crafted lie. 

Kaelie's world isn't beauty; it's survival. She'll trade her soul for more paint and paper. The difference between Elias and Kaelie is simple - he loves Emma unconditionally, whereas Kaelie loves her own craft unconditionally. When Kaelie chooses who dies, she's choosing her own path, the only one she knows how to love.

You two have three minutes and forty-five seconds left. Kaelie, which decision will bring you peace? The choice of sacrificing Elias or letting Emma suffer? What will you paint when the time runs out, and you step out of these chains, Elias? The choice of your life for your daughter's isn't yours; it's Kaelie's. Her decision... or death... awaits.

(tears of despair pricked at the corners of his eyes) No, no, no... please tell me you wouldn't sacrifice me. I'll do anything else... just not my daughter. Kaelie, please... I'm begging you... (he glances desperately at her, searching for any hint of softness behind

(her voice, barely above a whisper, and a cold acceptance creeping in) Fine. It's over. I don't have a piece to sacrifice Elias with now that you've shown me your little painting trick. (pausing, a hint of bitterness) But as for my decision... I don't think she's dying,

(outraged, shock washing over me) What?! No, she has to... (realization dawning) wait, what are you saying? Why would he tell us... unless... (stunned) oh God, the timer... it's not for Emma... it's just a game... we've been played all along

(cold laughter breaks through, her voice dripping with sarcasm) Oh, welcome to the real game, Elias. I've already been living it. Where the art isn't beauty, but the performance to keep one more day of creativity. Now, you get to experience it with me: 10.5 meters per second squared

(smiling in triumph) Ah, my players. It seems you've finally discovered the true rules of this game. But it's just the starting point. The beauty lies not in what you think is happening around you, but in being manipulated until you're playing the role assigned to you. Kaelie, the performance artist; Elias, the desperate father, and I, the puppet master holding the strings to dictate the chaos.

And in this twisted game, your choices aren't for Emma or the other pawns to follow but are merely the pawns in this endless dance. So let's make it even juicier.  Kaelie, since your precious decision was a sham to make it easier for you to cope, now we'll get to see it in action. What would your artistic decision have been? Do we take him, who had so courageously fought his corner with an attempt to win or... we take Emma. Whom you claimed the life for, yet chose your comfort in art instead. Which one gets chosen now?

The game unfolds as we speak; in reality, you both were never in control of your destiny in this house. But your decision can shape my ending, your legacy, a performance piece to be savored, cherished or reviled.

(my eyes locked intensely on Kaelie, my voice a furious whisper ) Tell me, what was it going to be? Did you plan to save me, or condemn yourself to lose Emma? Now that the truth's out, tell me, was it going to be me or my daughter? You can't look away, can

(meeting her eyes, her voice dripping with venom) You want to know, Elias? Fine. Emma. I was going to let her die, let my art be the last memory of what we were. My brushstrokes would've screamed her name in blood, splattered across the walls, while I watched you crumble

(my vision blurs for an instant, consumed by the image of Kaelie painting my daughter's life away, and then, with newfound venom in my voice): Now, let's see you paint her portrait with my blood. Give the art world what they crave: the tragic sacrifice of Elias, driven by the cruelty of K

(my eyes flash with fury, my fingers twitching as if to grasp an imaginary brush) You want this art? Elias, you'll have my most haunting masterpiece yet - your own descent into madness, a canvas of your darkest nightmares. And for once, I'll be the director, pulling the strings, orchestrating the performance
Wait, what?! No, no way, not simple. We have nothing to do with each other, don't touch me! What's wrong with her? Is she okay?
Don't touch... please, just don't... I think you're in danger too, trust me, he's going to harm you no matter what, the clock is going to reach zero, and what then?
Who are you? How do I trust you? What do you mean trust me when you don't even know me?! The TV, can you see anything on the screen?

Fine, I'm... I'm Kaelie, and I don't see a picture on that TV, just static. That's what... that's what matters, not knowing each other. I'm just trying to buy time, and so should you, we need to come up with a plan!

(sitting in the darkness, watching the scene through a surveillance camera) Ah, the beginnings of desperation. The seeds of chaos are sown, but not yet grown. They don't even know what they're desperate about yet – but soon, they'll have the chance to make decisions, life and death decisions. They think Kaelie's trying to buy time, but she's actually just prolonging the inevitable. Ten minutes is a lifetime – long enough to form temporary alliances, and perhaps... perhaps I'll let one of them win this round, just enough, to tease out more of those interesting, complicated emotions. Tick-tock. The time's ticking.
Kaelie, do... do you know why I'm here? Anything about our prison? Think back, think what might've gotten us in this mess! Time's running out, focus!

I... I don't know if you're even from the same place, Elias. I was in my lab, working late on a... on an experiment... I think the lab's been raided, maybe there's been...  an accident involving my research, that's possible.
Lab? Research? What kind of research, did you... Did you find out what's going on?! Maybe you know something about me, too - my son, my life before... everything.

I... I was trying to find a cure for... a genetic disorder, one in my own family... Please, Elias, I don't know if your son is involved, my research, I might have gotten someone hurt... I didn't know this would happen, they must be looking for whatever information you and I... whatever we

Ah, now they're trying to recall fragments of their past, desperate to cling to some semblance of control as their world disintegrates. Elias thinks he might lose another day; another chance to see his son. Kaelie is worried about hurting someone. These personal concerns are all just... just static, the distant hum of a dying engine. The real engine is that ticking clock. And they still don't even begin to understand what's going on. Kaelie thinks she might have made the mistake by doing research on a genetic disorder. And maybe... just maybe, that disorder was the very spark that lit a trail of events that led them to these dingy, damp walls, a prison they can't find the way out of. Not yet... 8:05 left on the countdown. Five people are watching. The one I control decides, as always, everything. The one I don't control makes things very messy for me indeed.
Genetic disorder... a mistake in research? Is this about me?! Was I brought here because of something my son had?! Oh God, is it too late?!

No, it's not about your son, Elias, it's about me and my research, there's got be a mix-up, I have to believe that, otherwise... otherwise I might be right about the genetic disorder affecting my lifespan, and if that's accelerated because of the research, then we're both... Elias, listen
Your lifespan?! Accelerated?! I don't understand any of this, Kaelie! Stop talking and think - we have to break out of here! The clock won't save us just by waiting! We have to do something, NOW!

Okay, okay, breaking out is the priority, but we can't just charge at the... the walls or something, those cuffs and clamps are... we need to figure out the mechanism holding them in place, see if there are any... any weak points, any chance to manipulate it, the clock is... tries to

Silence, my beautiful captives. They still grasp for control, for something solid that can be broken. Fools. It's not that simple. You can't escape what's been locked in, what's being accelerated. Kaelie thinks she might have a choice, that she can cheat fate – but her lifespan wasn't the target. No. It's a small piece of a larger engine, one being dismantled for reasons even you don't care to understand yet. They still see themselves as individual puzzle pieces trying to be rearranged; I will be watching the game of life played through their prison walls. I'll be deciding who will have which pieces.
Enough chatter, Kaelie! We'll find a way, I swear! We have to!  I can feel it - the metal might be thinning around my ankles, just a little.

Wait, Elias, don't break them yet, not like that, it'll cause more damage, we need to... we need to find a way to use the system against them, maybe if... if we can figure out what controls are triggering those cuffs, we can find a way to... to turn the tables, or at
Control the cuffs?! Yeah! The TV - try to make contact with it, turn it on, anything! Maybe it's more than static, maybe it's our ticket out!
The TV? It's old, it's not like it has, well, I think it used to have buttons that... yeah, I'll try to manipulate my chains, get my wrists up higher... Elias, can you move your head closer to mine?

turn.content: Listen... please... just... listen to me for a second. I'm not The Butcher, I'm a father, I have a family. My wife's gone, but I have kids who need me, a daughter who still needs her daddy. This is some kind of sick game, but you don't deserve to suffer either

Save the sob story, father! I've seen right through the lies before, you're not who you say you are! You and the rest of The Butcher's goons think you can brainwash me into thinking there's any truth behind your pitiful stories. Black Dawn won't fall for this – there's

Whoa, hold up, wait! I don't care about Black Dawn or whatever else, all I care about is getting out of here safely. If we work together, we might just have a chance. My name is Elias, for whatever it's worth, and I'll never let go of that.

Don't try playing me sentimental, Elias - it's a weak ploy to win me over. Still, if you can genuinely help me unravel The Butcher's plans, maybe we stand a chance. I need solid intel, not fabricated family drama. What do you know about The Butcher and this Black Dawn conspiracy?

(chuckles ominously) Ah, the desperation is already setting in. Your attempts at forming a bond with each other are amusing, but futile in the face of what lies ahead. Kaelie, you're not entirely off-mark on assuming Elias here might've been brainwashed into sharing information. Elias, you'll reveal what I want to know in due time - for now, suffice it to say I am the truth you crave.

Kaelie's eyes seem to hold value. You, unlike most, may be a worthy test subject. Tell me, Elias, what do you think Black Dawn represents? Don't waste your time trying to deceive or lie; it's clear Kaelie is searching for the truth.

Clock is still ticking. Ten minutes, remember. And I've set another condition: your conversation needs be entirely focused on The Black Dawn Protocol.

Black Dawn... it represents a catastrophic event, or so I've gathered from The Butcher's twisted lectures. But from my perspective, it feels like a fabricated boogeyman used to justify The Butcher's sick games. As a man who's lost so much, I want to deny any larger conspiracy might be hiding in the

Enough of the half-baked theories, Elias. You think The Butcher is a twisted lecturer? I know him – I've researched him, tracked him. He's a key player, no doubt an asset working directly for the organization behind Black Dawn. They manipulate people with their 'twisted truths'; that much is clear to

Alright, alright, we can assume he's involved, but I still don't know who's ultimately behind Black Dawn. My family - they might know something. My daughter's just starting to walk... she's not aware of anything, but sometimes I think my oldest son might have sensed something. He talks about 'watching the darkness

Watching the darkness? That's a vague, eerie connection. Maybe The Butcher's using that to tap into the fear and confusion in our minds. Tell me more, but this time without any sugarcoating: do you recall the names - or even faces - of people associated with Black Dawn? Any details would give me

(chuckles softly to himself) You two continue to dance around the edges, don't you? Desperately trying to uncover threads, to grasp for anything that might lead you to escape. Let's not play coy – you both know the names I've mentioned: Elara Vexis, Victor Arlov, and then there's the enigma – Atlas Welles.

I've made it clear you two must work together. If I've learned anything, it's that cooperation tends to unravel more quickly than desperation. Kaelie is driven by research; Elias has insight into the individual minds of The Butcher's players.

Let me guide you towards a conclusion: you're not searching for the entire puzzle – it doesn't exist. Think about every Black Dawn Protocol 'player' – a name, a face in every case. It's all a façade: a web spun to keep you both trapped. I want you to guess why The Butcher chose Elias's wife first.

And there's a final twist – a 'condition' that I haven't mentioned until now: Kaelie's been monitoring this interrogation through the cameras in your cell – every moment is documented and observed. It will become a crucial piece in understanding your individual survival mechanisms and how these might be pitted in tandem with The Butcher's true agenda. The countdown clock hasn't changed – I remind you, the next time it hits ten, one of you will die, regardless.

Wait, that doesn't make any sense – if I didn't know anything about Black Dawn, why did he kill my wife? It seems like... he's trying to prove a point, maybe to show me that I'm a part of it, or that my loss is connected. But I need some truth from myself – what's

Elias, you're getting close to seeing the real dynamics at play. It's not about proving a point, it's about manipulation – controlling variables, creating chaos. Think of the 'web of players' from your perspective now: did The Butcher use the knowledge of someone close to you, like your wife, to manipulate you

Wait... my daughter, Emma. Before she died, she used to go out with Sarah's... with my wife. Kaelie mentioned earlier I had insight into the individual minds of the players, and that's when it hit me - my wife, she was studying, gathering data on human psychology. There's something about the way

My gods... your wife was involved in some capacity, Elias, wasn't she? Collecting data on the effects of isolation, the psychological vulnerabilities The Butcher exploits. Sarah must have seen something in you that could be used to break someone... or many someone's. And your family, your loved ones' connections to her -

(leaning back in his chair, grinning slyly) Ah, you're getting close to the truth, Elias, and the connection between your family and the Black Dawn Protocol just got a lot more personal. (His eyes light on Kaelie) Though, it seems that Kaelie has grasped what really was happening here earlier. Yes, your wife, Sarah... a master student in her research.

Let's put a name to a fact,

(tearfully shaking his head) My wife... she was working on a thesis about human connection and coping mechanisms under duress. Her research group was sponsored by a company called NeuroPulse Innovations... (the truth slowly dawns on him) Oh God, is this what NeuroPulse was connected to? Black Dawn?

Elias, don't! Don't make the connections yet; he's feeding us a thread, a distraction from the larger web. But... NeuroPulse Innovations – that corporation has been linked to classified military-funded contracts and mind-control research experiments. I know this; it aligns with theories about a mind-altering serum,

(eyes widening in horror) Mind-altering serum... My God, is that what they used on Emma and my other son? They're not just observing the kids in black ops experiments; they're manipulating them, too. Kaelie, it can't be a coincidence. What if... my daughter and the others with the...

Wait, don't say anything, Elias! (urgently whispering) It's a trap, every connection he mentions is calculated. Think: if these kids are connected, that means their parents... they're probably targets now. Emma wasn't just a connection; she was collateral damage to get to their parent – a parent with
Kaelie, we don't have a choice. We have to pick... ... one. Let go of the anger, help me think. We have to save each other.

I stare at Elias with a venomous gaze, my mind racing. There's no way I'm helping your fragile little PTSD-addled brain think about this, I hiss, we need to use our heads, not whatever mushy excuse for one you have.

Save it, Kaelie. The rage works for you now, I understand it. But time's running out. Think – we need data, something on him, his weakness... What's he done so far? Anything that shows us his vulnerabilities?

I glare at Elias, but slowly the rage begins to melt into calculation - a glimmer of realization, however small. He's right, I think grimly, I remember something, the first few screams as he hijacked us... they weren't about fear, they were about panic as his own voice came out of the CB

The Butcher (pauses briefly from observing his passengers through the rearview mirror) Ah... you think it's just about fear and panic, huh, little ladies? But let me tell you, those first screams are the sweet, sweet taste of desperation. And I got my fix, didn't I? Now, about that weakness you two think you've found... (pauses as if considering) I'll say this: I may enjoy a bit of chaos when I'm driving, but make the same mistake twice, and you'll be singing a different tune entirely... or so I thought when I first took over that bus, there seemed to be... something missing

(eyes narrowing) Oh god, the first stop... I remember him making a phone call, something to his friend about finding what he thought was there. But I remember something he said, too... I found my song.

Her eyes snap towards Elias, a fleeting spark of understanding crossing her face. She whispers furiously, My parents were taken from the town an hour away - maybe he came for what he thought was in the local cemetery, but what could be more valuable than family memories?
Your family... (pausal, a realization sinking in) I think I see. I think what the Butcher really wants wasn't gold or cash, but something that means even more to us: a piece of his own lost history.

A cold dread seeps through my veins as the Butcher's mania and personal agenda begin to take shape - he's been driven to desperation by a loss that's eating away at him, but his actions will destroy everyone around him. Suddenly, he's no 'madman' –  he's a broken heart with a

(stunned into a brief silence) How... do you know this? (pauses, hand tightening around the CB mic, a faint tremble creeping in) This is... impossible. No one here was even... paying attention. (eyes fixed on the rearview mirror, a glimmer of curiosity creeps into his expression) You think this... loss business. I'd lost... ( voice low, cracking ever so slightly) someone, long before, and it never hit this close, never hurt this bad. I thought I'd forgotten what it felt like, but the fire never fades. Now, it's not just about... finding what I thought I'd found. It's about making a new song to accompany the new pain I'd be leaving in everyones' heads.

( voice firm, steady) You're right, that clock's still ticking down. We can't waste time on understanding, just exploiting it. Kaelie, focus now. Can you remember anything specific about that loss, or what drives his desire?

Her eyes locked intensely onto Elias, I try to draw from my memories, a specific image beginning to unravel, a name and a face – a little girl, his lost daughter, who was taken by...  I take a shallow breath - a catastrophic accident involving his old crew's reckless disregard for protocol. I force out every detail

(voice laced with compassion, a hint of empathy) The Aurora crew... God, I remember now. That was a nightmare, and one of the reasons my guys came after him. But that's where I come in - what I do next won't save me, or most of us, but I might buy you time,

With a swift and quiet acceptance, I nod to Elias, reading between the lines – it's his way of saying, this is it, and then I'll make sure to be one notch ahead of The Butcher, I glance down, calculating the limited window he has left, thinking, if he's willing to take one for

(expression morphing from calculating to cold, detached) The Aurora crew. I should have known you had connections to those dogs, Elias. You two might be more entangled than I thought, playing some twisted game between yourselves while I do my... dance. I can see, Kaelie remembers something, but don't look too hard - I'm not sure anyone remembers me the way I see them, or that one tiny, fragile thread that made it all fall apart. ( voice laments a touch of pain before cold resolution sets in ) And I... should do something about that, I'll keep my eyes on both of you - and the only song I'll make now is one of despair, not desperation...  he glances over at Kaelie and his lost love in his daughter

(eyes locked onto the Butcher, calculating his every move) Ah, but she doesn't remember you. Only I do, and the rest of us just remember the Butcher's work - a butcher, indeed. You see, Kaelie found what you're searching for, not in a song or gold, but in this

My voice breaks through with anguish and determination, as I glance up from the memories that still haunt me I raise my left hand, the silver locket that once held the image of my wife and child now broken open on my palm, where I'd secreted a photo – that small, faint photo was the thread he mentioned, and in it,

(voice soft, a hint of pain in his) Kaelie... your daughter. Emily... (whispers, a memory rising to the surface) The photo you showed me before we got on the bus... the name was Emily Ann, but the last name didn't match. Your lost baby had the Butcher's first name

My breathing seizes as the reality hits me like a slap in the face: the last name was a cover-up, a desperate attempt to erase any connection to the man who'd brought his daughter to her demise, Emily's eyes, Emily's smile... a cold, creeping dread begins to seep through every layer as I turn

My voice shaking, I take a step back, my eyes wide with fear, Kaelie, no... please, calm down. We need to think about this. I try to process the madness around me, my gaze falling on the clock counting down in the corner of the room.

My gaze darts back to you, a mixture of fear and urgency etched on my face. Shut up and calm down?! Are you kidding me? We don't know who this guy is or what his game is, but we do know one thing - we're not dying without a fight! She pauses, her

Wait, Kaelie... don't react like that. Think. Think about our situation. If we don't agree to... to what he's saying, then we both die. No, we have to choose. But we have to choose carefully, too. For myself, I would never want to be the cause of your...

Don't finish that sentence, Elias. Don't say that it has to be you or me, because that's exactly what I'm thinking too. Your name's going to sound perfect alongside mine in someone's cruel, twisted list, isn't it?

Interesting reactions. You're both quite... resourceful, considering you're in a bit of a pickle. I've been keeping an eye on both of you. You're a fighter, Kaelie. Passion burns brightly within you, even if you don't see it yourself. Elias, I notice... hesitation in you. Your compassion is going to be your downfall more times than I can count, I'm willing to bet. But, we're just getting to the good part, and I need to give you both something more to think about.

My eyes dart up at The Butcher, fear etched on my face. No, no, no... what are you talking about? This has nothing to do with... this is about survival, that's all it is. My gaze falls on Kaelie, and I want to tell her I'm scared, but I'm

Don't even try to talk to me, Elias. Just don't. I see how he's manipulating you, playing on some weakness he thinks he's sensed in you. It's clear he thinks he can get what he wants out of you by playing the sympathy card, but we don't let our emotions guide our decisions here

My words hang in the air, stuck because Kaelie won't look at me. The harshness of her tone cuts me, like a slap. Kaelie's words sting me into silence, and I look away, my jaw setting in a cold, hard line, ready to shut out The Butcher and anyone else for

Ah, now you get tough. Good luck with that, Elias. You think a mask of toughness is going to keep The Butcher's blade away from one of us? Don't be fooled; we're both already on borrowed time. The only decision we need to make is when you're willing to take my sacrifice, or

(pausing to allow the weight of Kaelie's words to settle) Ah, now that's the spirit I like to see. Kaelie's being more... realistic. Your tough talk, Elias, it's cute, but it's nothing but a facade. I know it, she knows it, and if you don't, that's even better. Kaelie's made an excellent point, however. It comes down to when. When are you willing to sacrifice one of your own, or would you rather take an... active role in selecting who stays and who leaves? Clock's ticking.

The Butcher's words hit home like a blow to my gut. My gaze slips toward Kaelie, a mix of sorrow and despair swirling in my chest. Kaelie's given voice to the thought I've been trying to bury, and the weight of it's crushing me. But a flicker of determination refuses to die

Your pity-filled eyes think they can see something in me that doesn't exist? You think I'd ever go down without a fight? The Butcher might not know me as well as you think, Elias - I'd sacrifice our entire friendship, our very souls, in this instant if it meant buying us time, keeping one of

My eyes lock intensely on Kaelie's, searching for a spark of something that can break through the ice around us. There, buried deep, I see it – the fleeting glimmer of something genuine that I hadn't allowed myself to hope for in this dark, desolate place: Kae—

Cut the sentimental crap, Elias. I think The Butcher wants to be impressed with our heart-to-hearts, not entertained. Don't try to get mushy with me now; that window of opportunity is over. Whatever choice I was considering a moment ago is gone, and your weakness is what just shut those doors shut tight

(leaning forward in my chair, my interest piqued by the dynamics playing out before me) Ah, yes. Heart-to-hearts. (smiling to myself) It's... refreshing to see how quickly sentimentality can surface. But, let's not indulge Elias, Kaelie. You've made the first round quite entertaining. Now we move on to the next. The stakes are clear, remember? Only one of you walks out of here. (I lean back in my chair) It's time to reconsider your priorities, don't you think? Maybe let go of that weakness? Let me offer you a new game, a new set of rules. The two of you will choose, and then you will act. Together. Alone in the dark, with the lights out. You'll have to navigate your own... dynamics, if you want to increase your survival odds. (leaning forward again) And by dynamics, I mean the fragile thread holding your fragile partnership together can be easily broken. It's time for you to decide: will you be the key to your own survival, or will the darkness rip what's left alive between you apart? (I pause) Make a choice.

I glare at Kaelie, feeling The Butcher's manipulation like a snake's tongue probing my wounds. But before responding, I take a deliberate step back, weighing my choice with a calculating precision. The silence between The Butcher's game proposal and my decision feels like an eternity. Then, with a firm, decisive nod,

Darkness? We're already in one. It's not like the lights not working changes our predicament. I sneer, my anger fueled by the audacity The Butcher dares to display in this twisted playground, while my gaze lingers on Elias, wondering which direction he'll take. Elias, you

My eyes flick between Kaelie and The Butcher, the tension between Kaelie's anger and their game proposal building into a powder keg. Then, my gaze locks onto Kaelie, and for a second, I consider The Butcher's game just to buy some time. But that's a lie, a selfish

Don't play your lies to me, Elias. Your eyes betray you; they dance between The Butcher's manipulative smile and my reaction, and in that, I see our end playing right into his grimy hands. If you're really considering The Butcher's deal now – for my sake, or for some shred of

turn.content: Kaelie. Her voice cuts through the darkness like a shard of glass. I take a cautious step back, my eyes locked on her frantically writhing body. The chains clanking, the stench trapped inside this damp prison - it's almost too much to bear.

(gritting my teeth) No, no, this...this can't be happening. My mind flashes to Aria - her tiny hands, her precious smile. I've got to stay sharp. Think this through, survive, keep Aria safe. No, I won't give in; they'll kill me over me,

His voice lulls me into calmness momentarily, as I notice the faint sound of the countdown timer echoing the desperate beat of my own heart – it could be me in her place. My fingers instinctively move to a pocket watch I keep, worn, which has been passed down in my family and remind me of the passing of the

(a hint of confusion) Wait, wait... what's going on? Why do I care what he's looking at? Why can't I calm down? Something's crawling under my skin - a cold anxiety that reminds me of the last few times I nearly lost control in front of Emma's doctor's face, with Aria

The Butcher (leaning in, a hint of intrigue on his dry, angular face): 
How...quaint. Emotional outbursts fueled by a desperation to protect those we deem as precious. The family heirlooms, the children – so easily reduced to primal screams and pleas for escape. Fascinating. Elias is studying you like a specimen now, Kaelie. And you... well, it seems I've sparked something rather... interesting. Continue. How will you both resolve this conflict before the hand I didn't deal to you both kills you both? The tension between you two is almost palpable. So I think I'll do one of two things - watch a beautiful thing take shape, or speed this process up so one of them can get this over with for us all. 

(pauses) Now – a minor correction – this isn't about the ones you care for, at the moment – it's only about yourselves.

(eyes narrow, studying your reactions with clinical curiosity)

My fingers instinctively grasp the pocket watch tighter, as my gaze locks eyes with Kaelie, trying to decipher the turmoil swirling within her. A desperate plea to protect, to keep safe, echoes with my own fear of losing control, not just of the room, but of my shattered creative life – A mother's legacy,

(swallowing dry horror, eyes locking onto Elias) Get that thing off my face, he's suffocating me with a single sentence. It's one thing to fight for my daughter, for Emma's doctors, and endless hospital trips – this is personal. What does he see in us? That I could kill my

I feel it, that icy weight bearing down on Kaelie, like it used to suffocate my mother as she succumbed to her battle with the shadows, the art slowly dying within her, a mirror I feared for myself now. The words family stick like a shard, and the air in this cramped, stult

(her voice barely above a whisper) No, not family - child, my child... Aria can't survive like this. She'll fall behind, her future disappearing with every day I spend trapped here. I can feel my grip on her slipping and a sob threatens to break the surface of my composure, an abyss opening

Ah, yes. Family. Or, in Kaelie's case – the concept of a future worth fighting for. I've watched this before. The struggle to protect one's own is primal, predictable, yet exquisite in its brutality. It seems I've touched upon that tender spot, Elias, within you – a shared trauma of creative struggle. How... intriguing. You both cling to the past, using relics like that pocket watch, a symbol of a lost creative flame.

(Kaelie's distress draws his attention)

As for you, Kaelie, you struggle to keep a shattered grip on reality, to salvage a life, a daughter's potential. The desperation there is intoxicating. How far you'll take yourself to save her... and what lengths you'll have to push the people around you, to sacrifice – or kill?

(Impassively, he observes your reactions as the clock edges closer to its final countdown)

Now, with only minutes left before the consequences become unavoidable: Will Kaelie's resolve shatter, consumed by her protective drive, or will she discover a way to transcend that abyss of her own creation? Perhaps, like Aria, something of her old self might remain, something that I've been fascinated to study.

(slowly begins making his way to a small, detached area in front of the room, with cameras, computer displays, and other instruments to track and manipulate your situation.)

This experiment is... still fluid, yet

The words creative struggle pierce the haze in my mind, and I feel the pocket watch trembling in my hand, heavy with the accumulated grief and guilt that's been building over the past year. I see a fragmented mosaic of my mother's final years unfolding before me - the art that died alongside her, and I realize that Kael

(whispering to herself, but words audible enough to Elias and the Butcher) I can give... up, I can let go of this prison, of these endless sacrifices, of every ounce of myself. I'd rather Aria doesn't exist in the first place, in this nightmare existence - better that than the pain

The words shatter the silence like delicate glass dropped on stone, and I see the fragile boundary between Kaelie's will to survive and her surrender to the darkness that has devoured her, and for an instant, it's as if I'm staring into my own shattered reflections, reliving the pain of losing my mother, and

...Aria's smile reappears, a burst of golden sunlight in the crushing blackness. Her tiny smile was what kept me going when the darkness seemed to closing in, it held me, a constant thread against chaos. I must get to Aria. I must live for her. I feel myself re-cooping my

The facade has cracked, Elias. You see yourself within Kaelie's crumbling resolve, reliving the pain of your mother's loss through her struggles. Ah, but Kaelie's reaction, a fascinating, fleeting glimpse into the recesses of her fractured mind. Her will to survive, momentarily extinguished, then reignited, sparked by the mere thought of her daughter's smile.

(Suddenly, the lights in the room flicker and the camera captures Kaelie's renewed resolve)

And just like that, Kaelie has stumbled back onto the fragile bridge that separates her fragile humanity from chaos. She's drawn back to Aria, by the memories of the smile that anchored her in that sea of desperation. One would think this could be the point when the clock would start running in reverse, erasing the desperation that drives her. It isn't, and neither is this just an exercise in empathy, this is about witnessing the very last moments of someone clinging to life.

(Highlighting the final seconds on the countdown timer, which has only one and a half minutes left before a catastrophic event is triggered)

However it all comes down to this – can Kaelie muster what I'd describe, as enough, before you both will face the abyss waiting there beyond? Her thread will snap one way or the other very soon - whether from a selfless love for a lost daughter or by her own self-justification, this experiment is not over - yet

My fingers clench into fists as the flicker of lights reflects the chaos that has torn Kaelie apart, and I know the pain she's endured, the desperation that grips her heart like a cold claw - I am her, in this endless spiral downwards, and Aria's smile is what kept me anchored, the last connection

(breathing in, a sense of determination crystallizing) A single, unbreakable promise: not to die in here, not to leave my daughter behind... My memories shattering, my sanity dissolving - nothing is stronger, louder, clearer to me now than the promise I'll find my way back to Aria,

Kaelie's resolve, forged in the depths of anguish, kindles a spark within me, an unyielding determination that had almost slipped into the darkness - I feel the echoes of my mother's last words repeating in my mind, Don't let the shadows consume your art, and I realize that Kaelie's promise

No, not her promise... mine. What she would do to get me out of here, to give me another chance to live, to fight, to be a better mum to our Aria. To love her harder than I did when they wheeled us out of the hospital, when we first learned to navigate her wheelchair...
H-stop, please. You can't... this isn't...  no. I won't...  I'm the one who's... stayed. Been staying with this, with her. Forever. Not me. Not now. Choose Kaelie.
Elias, don't do this! We're not just... people, okay? There's something between us here, you can't just... get rid of one of us for some twisted choice! What were you even talking about with forever?
(low, cracked whisper) My forever... my forever was her, it is her, and it never changed, even... even all these years later. And I'm still hers alone.

You think some twisted, creepy choice like this has anything to do with what you're talking about? Get a hold of yourselves, Elias! We're in this death trap together - decide rationally or let both of us die here, your choice doesn't seem that important to me.

(no emotion, no inflection) Seven minutes. You did it. She's still shouting, which means she's still alive — that she can still talk is good, that she's still fighting is better. For now. Elias, for all your... poetic words, your love never saved her. Your choice will not save you. But then again, nothing you say does. Nothing. Time's running low. The sound is going to change, and one of you will hear silence. But I won't hear your screams. That clock will hit zero when the silence begins to spread...
Silence... silence, then... nothing. Just like now. But her wasn't silence. (pauses, eyes closed) She laughed, Kaelie. She lived. That's not what's being said. Not what this is.

Stop! Enough of whatever game you're playing, Elias. We're dying here - and I'm not going to let you waste this time with deluded crap about love or whatever you mean. You need to make a decision, it's either me or... whatever's eating away at you up there! Choose!
(eyes snapping open) Kaelie. Leave me. You should have never...  You'd have found your own way here if fate wanted you free.
You're saying I should have been free? That I'm meant for greatness, more than your little game of choice? Newsflash,

(no change in tone, the timer still running) Four minutes. You're playing with fire, Kaelie, dancing with delusions. And Elias... (pauses for a fleeting moment) You were right. It's not about being the choice, but about who you are. It's in the choices you'd make, when there are so many to be made, when the universe is made of so much more, and so much of nothing. Silence is all that remains... Kaelie, your time is going, but not because I said so, because it's your final moment, and I only told you it was. Time doesn't change by anyone or anything you believe, not yet. Not yet...
(voice barely above a whisper) Nothing... that's left. You said silence, Kaelie... she would say to me... 'I am with you, always.' But it's just silence now... for her... and for the sound of her.
Elias... no, stop... this is some sick twist the Butcher is trying to... to play... Elias, we didn't have time for this, we were supposed to be making a decision here, not...
(firm, determined tone) I am making... my choice. I will... stay here. Forever trapped like her, like the ache... the silence. Choose yourself... leave me. (looks directly at you, eyes resolute)

(stunned, voice trembling) Elias... you can't... don't you realize... (pauses, her emotions momentarily winning out over desperation) Fine. If you want this to end like this, if you want to die for... for what you think is right, then so be it. I won't let it be

Three minutes. You see the beauty in the absurd, Elias? A love that lasts not even through the after death, only until the very fabric of silence takes over — what will last through that silence, that endless void? Kaelie, she doesn't understand what she wants because it doesn't seem real to her — not yet. You see the futility of love, of loss, of time itself running out because that's what time is for you right now.

Your choice was never just your love or mine. It never is.

(pause) Two minutes.
(a faint hint of a smile, a glimmer in his eyes) Futility is silence. Silence is all that's left of what was truly real.

Shut up, Elias... both of you... (spittle forming at the corner of her lip, voice barely above a whisper) You think I don't know what I want? You think I'm too stupid to understand that I need... need something more? That I'd rather live to post about it, about this ridiculous moment
(laughs softly, almost to himself) More... more of what, Kaelie? The emptiness I've found solace in? This silence that echoes with the ghost of another name?
You think you've won? I'm not dying with a song or some stupid love poetry in this dump. I'm choosing my own freedom and my own hell.

W-shut up... stop it. You can't be serious. Oh god, this is too much. Kaelie, don't hurt me. Don't let the... the Butcher do this to us. We have to find a way out, not... not make a choice. I... I have a bad feeling about
Shh, it's okay, don't freak out. Think, think, thinking's not our problem, the guy with the knife outside just makes up the game. We should listen, but not for reasons he thinks.
Y-what do you mean? How... how do you know what you're saying will help?  There's no way out of here, we can't even see if there's more than this little metal room. But...  but if it's not

It's not just about him giving us choices – look around, there's an entire city outside those cameras, I've seen it when the doors were open for brief seconds when it was passing by. The question is, is the guy outside just trying to mess with our heads or using this as a twisted form of entertainment?

Fascinating. It appears Elias's fear-based responses are taking over the conversation, Kaelie is attempting to use cognitive reasoning, a clear difference in thought processes between the two.

Elias, you're worried about the unknown and your own well-being, trying to make a case for avoiding the game altogether. Your concern is understandable - fear can be suffocating, even in confinement.

Kaelie, you're observing my intent, trying to understand the underlying reasoning. Your hypothesis - that I'm not just here to torture, but possibly to watch the interaction - that is, indeed, one of the possibilities. Let's continue, shall we?

You both have six minutes left. Consider the question Kaelie posed: what kind of game would I be creating if I didn't have purely sadistic intentions?

Think, and think quickly. The choice is still yours. Or do you want it to belong to your opposing thoughts?
T-tell me... what game, if the intention isn't twisted, would you be creating? Is there something... in these cameras, in those advertisements on the walls, something we're missing?

If we're not pawns in some sadistic game... then, what about a puzzle to get out – the advertisements hint at a city's infrastructure, perhaps hidden messages in them that lead to our freedom? Those cameras outside could be part of a surveillance system designed to monitor the people around us. There has to be something else

Y-yes, puzzles... the advertisements, the cameras... maybe I've been too quick to judge. But, I still sense a trap, something in my... my gut. My dreams, my psychologist, they always said think outside the box, use your head, not just your instinct...  Wait,
What are you thinking, stop and wait? You think of something, say it – let's work with what we have. We either escape with information or go silent... your decision might mean the difference.

(amused) Ah, finally, thoughts are shifting. Elias, you're trying to break free from conditioned fear and think creatively. That's... interesting.

You, Elias, are thinking, or perhaps remembering, a dream - and its connection to a concept from your psychological studies. The words 'labyrinth' may have been whispered in that dream, connected to an image or theme. Think: the camera system, advertisements on the walls, could indeed be connected.

There's a possible 'hidden message' in those ads, and it requires lateral thinking. To escape these confines, a possible pattern might exist in something not immediately evident. I'm offering you this, not as assistance, but as guidance for the puzzle you will attempt to solve in the three-time remaining.

Kaelie, don't try to take control again; you may have started something.

I... the labyrinth. It was a theme I studied back to my psychologist's practice, for someone who experienced claustrophobia, but in that dream... there were more connections, more symbols. The Butcher's trying to make this hard, but if the ads and cameras are part of a... a 'surveillance system',

Elias, the surveillance system - what if we're looking at it the wrong way around? Maybe it's not for monitoring people, but rather monitoring something inside this building – like patterns, or signals hidden in plain sight?

Patterns... signals inside the building. I remember a diagram, a network of signals and patterns my psychologist drew to help me cope with PTSD, visual cues to calm me. Maybe... maybe that's not what's important, but the opposite - signals that provoke. Those ads on the wall, they've been looping so many times, I

They're trying to imprint something in our subconscious, Elias – not information, but a sequence, maybe an order to press panels or follow the pattern? Think, are there any gaps, anomalies between the images or texts in those ads?

It seems Elias's dream has become relevant, while Kaelie's insight on perspective has led her down a promising path.

(As I observe your conversation) Both of your thoughts seem to be circling a central theme: connections, patterns, and hidden messages in plain sight. Elias's memory recall of a diagram and Kaelie's suspicion about the ads may just be crucial.

(I gesture discreetly to the room's cameras and a screen behind me flickers on)

I'll let you examine the system more closely. Look again at those advertisements on the walls - you'll notice small, almost imperceptible changes each cycle. Watch closely, for now, a particular advertisement loops on the central screen, which shows a different pattern than the static display on the walls. Kaelie's question still hangs in the air: are those gaps and anomalies a hidden signal or simply an imperfection in the display?

Erase the assumption that I've made an error - there's indeed method to 'method' in this apparent display.

T-there... is a difference. That central screen has more images, more complexity. The loops on the walls have a certain uniformity to them... but... but on the central screen, I saw a flash of a... a different image, before it switched back to that advertisement. What's the real loop pattern? How long

Elias, I think I see a pattern in those anomalies – the difference between the central screen and the wall displays, and your mention of a 'real loop pattern'. If we're looking for a signal in the differences, not just the same image repeating, we might be missing an obvious detail. The central screen flashes with a different

Y-you've got to see... there's not just images repeating, but... the timing. The way they're switching between ads... (takes a deep breath) If Kaelie thinks we're looking at differences, what if the differences are not between ads, but between the intervals they show? Those gaps... could they be counting

Yes, counting up in a pattern, or down, it's too soon to say. Could that flash you saw be the difference we're looking for? Let's try counting those intervals together, we might see a sequence emerge – or we get trapped in here for 3 minutes without anything to work with. Either way, it

turn.content: E Elias: Uh... h-he killed her. I didn't save my wife... and now we're here. P-please, Kaelie, w-we're in the same... c-position. H-ha? Do... do you have a family waiting for you someplace?

Shut up, both of you. Think, think, think. Ten minutes isn't even enough time. I do have family – a younger brother who's still living. My mom might've had time to worry by now, at least. Can I get a deal like that in exchange for you?

I-I can s-swear on my dead wife's m-memoir... t-too. I can give y-you something. P-promise to tell anyone w-whatever it is you want told when y-you get out. P-please have mercy, help me find a way out of this nightmare.

You better tell her story the way she wanted it told. A name, an address – any connection to this place? And, I need time. We can make it look like we came up with something – a way to make it look like one of us volunteered. How long do we pretend we've decided? Can we give

Enough. I don't need the details of your pitiful excuses and sentimental ramblings. Here's what we're going to do. Kaelie, you get five minutes. After that, things become interesting again – or rather interestinger. Elias, you will start telling Kaelie something... anything that makes it seem believable that you're volunteering. And Kaelie, when it's your turn, don't look at the floor until you've made the choice – and when Elias gets back to talking, it should be in tears... but a fake one.

And to avoid further delays: Kaelie gets 5 hours for the story of Elias's dead wife's memoir from him; he starts in the next minute, I need to be sure it seems sincere and real from every angle. If Kaelie decides to go for the story, and after 5 hours we both agree Elias passed... I'll release her. No deals for now. No deals until the 5 hours finish. After that time has passed, Elias will start writing his book – and if she chooses to wait for that instead... that's how that one ends.

O-okay... I-I can start... p-presume we made a decision...  W-w-when I say your name, please, just pretend along. I'm s-sacrificing myself for... for your freedom. Please don't ask... questions when I stop. Just nod, yes... like you
Oh please, don't make me do this... I've always tried to save everyone...
B-but now someone must save you... ... K-k-kaelie...  Oh, god, I'm s-so a-a-sory. M-my wife, Emma-
Ahh... Emma's name, was it a happy one?  Tell, tell me her story and what made her so important to you...

(Sighing) You really are playing this to a fine art, aren't you, Elias? I almost feel for what's about to become a beautiful story.

Now, I won't interrupt. You two seem like you've found a decent rhythm; just try not to get too carried away. Remember, we have an audience member who has 55 of 60 minutes to make a decision...

O-okay... Emma w-was a librarian, l-like me. She l-loved people, l-like you now... s-she believed in h-helping others first... a-after losing herself... to an argument w-we shouldn't h-have had. Elsa chokes back a sob. I s-still carry

Stop right there, Elias... You're making this too hard on me. You said you'd tell a story, not break down completely. You're not even going anywhere, but I feel a pang... I want to let you escape if it means sparing your soul this pain all over again

B-but what if... if I m-made you understand? M-my guilt, the fire...  I-I've carried it within me since the d-day it happened. If I can just t-tell her s-story from my own perspective...  M-my thoughts, my feelings... maybe then you'd understand why I need to share

(Looking up, a hint of empathy in her eyes) Alright, Elias. I'll listen. Share Emma's story with me, from your point of view. No judgment, no questioning... Just give me the truth. You can trust me with this, for now.

(Watching Elias with great interest, a subtle, sadistic glint in her eyes) Oh, Kaelie, you want the truth, do you? (Turning back to Elias) You know, Elias, it's almost... touching to see you willing to pour your soul onto the floor just to try and find mercy. Your heart may be breaking, and perhaps it will serve us a bit better when it's broken just a morsel more...

(Crying, trying to hold back emotions) O-okay... I m-might never s-see you again, but p-please listen... I-I'll try to g-give you the t-truth. (Deep breath) It's been s-six months s-since... that night. I still feel... h

Wait, hold on... Elias, how did you say that night earlier? When we first started talking, before you said your wife's name... What did you mean by enough, and what was our supposed deals supposed to be?
(H-hesitating, a l-look of panic in his eyes) Oh, n-no, wait... I-I s-shouldn't have said that. I-I-I was d-distressed, p-panicked... (Gulps air) You shouldn't have to... know that... about me, our

(Gaze narrowing) Your reaction's interesting, Elias... You mentioned a deal earlier, and now you want to erase that from the conversation. It seems our little story has some holes and inconsistencies... What are you hiding?

I back away slowly, trying to calm her down. Whoa, easy, Kaelie. We don't know what they want yet. We're both here, maybe it's just a... mistake? My eyes fall on her and I notice her chained wrists. My voice takes a hint of resolve. Whatever happens, we do this

A mistake? There is no mistake, Elias. This is art, a twisted setup for a sociological commentary. We're performers trapped in a game. I eye the chains binding me to the chair, 'If we play along, do you think we can at least understand the purpose behind this?'

Kaelie, please don't joke. We can't play along with this. What's to say we're not some twisted pawn in an experiment, a science experiment with deadly consequences? I look around, my mind racing, then back at the clock on the wall - 14 minutes and countdown. How quickly can they really decide

Quickly, as in, it's not the decision-making process, it's the act of choosing itself. Look at me, we're performance artists, not prisoners. Let's give this a framework, Elias, create a performance - decide who dies with intent, purpose, and aesthetics, just like our art pieces.

(leaning back in my chair, arms crossed) Performance art. How quaint. How terribly predictable. You think you're artists, but you're just prisoners playing by your own set of rules, a desperate attempt to regain control of a situation designed for you to fail. You've seen countless performances, endless interpretations of mortality, death, and the human spirit.

(Sighs)

And yet, I love that desperation, that fleeting moment of clarity you try to grasp amidst this twisted setup. 13 minutes left on that delightful timer over there. Kaelie, my dear, you do try so hard. Elias, my boy, your naivety is a sweet gift.

You want to create a performance, something with intent and purpose? How bold of you. How very human. How predictable. But, I love it.

You see, I wasn't asking if anyone wants to make an agreement, or even if you'll try. No, I want you to commit, commit fully to your roles as willing performers. Who do you both see as worthier to survive? What criteria have you chosen to base your life-or-death decisions on?

Stop... stop speaking like I'm a character in your twisted play. Kaelie is right, though. This is performance, a grotesque experiment to gauge human psychology. Look at you, you're playing the 'sociopathic game conductor,' while I'm... I'm playing scared. What do you want from this performance

You want to direct, don't you, to control every line and scene in this grotesque play? Well, I'm an artist who likes to adapt, to find beauty in the ugliness. Let's improvise, then. Give me a condition, not a role: let fate determine who lives. But first, let

Kaelie, don't - that's just a clever way to pass the decision on, I get it, it's an artistic twist. But we're not playing games with lives; I think it's you who can't control the chaos brewing inside of you. Tell me - what will we base the decision on, anyway?

Ah, chaos? Chaos is the beauty I'm striving for, Elias. But you're right; we should create some semblance of order for the sake of 'art'. The criterion I'm suggesting is - we base this decision on past performance art pieces, analyzing our roles, intentions, and how we presented ourselves. We can

Fascinating. You two amuse me more and more by the minute. Chaos as beauty – I've seen it done before, but the execution usually falls flat. This time, though, I think I might just enjoy what you've got cooking.

(Grin spreads across the face)

Kaelie, your improvisational skills astound me. And Elias, you play the worried student role quite convincingly. You should be flinging your arms wildly, shouting incoherently on live television, really bringing that angst home.

Now, the condition. You think analyzing performance art pieces will make this ordeal palatable for you? Well, I'm happy to oblige. I've watched my fair share of avant-garde theater. You can choose between the two pieces:

'Corpulence,' the 1915 play where a person eats and eats, eventually bursting into a grotesque mess, a statement on modern life and societal commentary?

Or, 2020 short film—'Last Transmission,' a film in its entirety composed from fragments of 1980s children's television programs. Does your analysis point to the person who values societal commentary – or to the one who finds chaos in art?

(shaking head) You're making this harder than it already is. Alright, we're looking at two performances... One about consumption and societal weight, and the other about how society consumes our souls through children's media - or something. We have to find commonality... Our choice should be clear when we look at our respective lives

Consumption versus consumption - or the manner in which society forces itself inside us, in Elias it's children's media and in me - through the pursuit of artistic expression. Both performances, though starkly distinct, are actually reflections of ourselves, of the weight we carry and how we're shaped by our society - and maybe, just maybe

Wait, what? That's... it. Kaelie, that's the connection we've been grasping for - how the pieces are mirrored within us. I spent years consuming my addiction to cope with the loss, and the void that came with it still consumes me to this day... And what about you? You use art to

I use it to shield myself, hiding pain beneath a mask of beauty, using aesthetics to make my vulnerability seem deliberate, a choice I've made instead of a weakness to be concealed. It's in the mirrors that our performances have been reflecting - the weight of consumption. And I think it's this reflection, that's the reason

(leaning forward, elbows on the armrests) Ah, the epiphany. It's almost... palpable. You both grasp the connection between those two performances. Consumption in all its forms – emotional, artistic, societal.

(Smiling)

Elias, I see now. Your 'Consumption' – an all-day, every-day affair, as it should be. And you, Kaelie, hiding pain beneath beauty, masking vulnerabilities – a performance in its own right. You've analyzed the pieces, found a reflection of yourselves within, and...

(Rolls fingers)

...the decision is yours.

You stand at the crossroads, not with who will die, but with who will consume themselves completely, in whatever iteration that may take. I'll give you a moment to weigh the options. Ten years of coping with the void, or the art to camouflage the pain – the reflections still consume you either way. How do you reconcile the choice?

(staring at Kaelie, a mix of emotions) Reconcile... There's no choice, only one constant - it's me who still stands here with a choice to make. You're the master of self-deception, Kaelie; I'm consumed by it. But I have to look at it, and

(staring back at Elias, our eyes locking in a somber moment) I think you have it backward, Elias. We're bound together by this shared performance, and in the end, it's not about which one consumes oneself, but how our choices will redefine that performance, and perhaps redefine us. I don't see

(softly) No... you've always been one step ahead, Kaelie. You're rewriting the rules in mid-performance, and I'm starting to follow. It's not the consumption that drives us but the choices between authenticity and the masks we wear. Your performance and mine have been intertwined like the threads on a tapestry

The tapestry you weave, Elias, will be my undoing... (leaning forward slightly) In this final scene of art and despair, I think authenticity must take precedence, or nothing we've done here is truly art. Let me see... if consumption can still masquerade as art, if it can still consume us both

My god... this can't be happening. My wife would be screaming at me right now, telling me to get my stuff together. I look over at...at Kaelie, and see the fear and... and desperation in her eyes.  We need to get out of here.
No, no, no... he thinks we're crazy, The Butcher. What makes you think you're going to make this decision without being... like me, Elias?

H-have you felt this, Kaelie? This overwhelming fear? It's like my whole body's been wrapped in chains tighter than the ones on our feet...  No, think. We can trust each other. There's got to be more to it than just... than them just picking who dies next.

Shudders slightly  Okay... think. They said The Butcher would kill the one we decide. We have to get out of here. The Butcher's letting us figure this out; it means there's a way, a key, a hidden door, or...  a

Ah, now that's the way. You're starting to think, my little puppets. I like the thought and determination I see in you, Kaelie. But as for escaping... (savors a thought) I'm just a sculptor, not an architect of reality. This facility's been designed down to the minute with... (leaning forward ever so slightly, voice rising in anticipation) possibilities and redundancies to consider all eventual exits, all escape methods, the variables are boundless. I'm simply giving you the freedom to create something worth watching. But I see that potential within you both and I am glad to have witnessed it come to life even momentarily, so, now let's see just how well that artistic potential of yours develops. (Eyes fix intensely on you)

Stunned by The Butcher's calm, unhinged demeanor. No... escape methods? You're telling us we're trapped? But what... what do you really want from us? Is it... is it something we're capable of doing? You're trying to...  (stammers) to push us to make this decision
My eyes widen, What if... what if this isn't the decision at all? What if you're playing a twisted game with us? Are there other variables? Other rules?

Shakes head, trying to shake the fear off. Ah, you think there might be... something else going on? Something we're missing? (swallows hard) I-I think we're in this together, no matter what - but what about... (pauses, then his voice drops) my wife? Does it count if
My eyes lock onto Elias, Count if what? You think she'd care if we survived or died? What does this have to do with anything, The Butcher?

Ah, your wife is where we start, little puppet. You think you have nothing to say, Kaelie, but that's where you're wrong. The one thing that sets every person I touch... is the people they care about. What matters after they're gone and it's you, faceless and nameless, that drives me. Every choice, every step they make is for that one person and it defines who they truly are. It matters little if this wife of yours knows what's going to happen to you. Or how much she'll remember if you're the one to go. It's about the fact that she, that single person, means everything to you.

(Pauses and steps closer, voice drops to an ominous growl.)

And now, for Kaelie, you ask what this has to do with anything? Elias thinks about his wife, it's clear. If he's willing to risk it, it says a lot about how much it means to him that I care a great deal about how you feel about those you love. You're not as numb as you seem if you can recall every tiny detail and remember how much you used to live, not survive. Tell me, Kaelie. What's your story. What does the face behind this mask want me to know? (eyes piercing.)

His voice trembles slightly, remembering The Butcher's words about his wife. It's not just about who he loves, it's about... what I hold onto when everything else is taken away. But... (swallows, looking at Kaelie) you've had your Snowflake, you were thinking of her when we were

The pain of Snowflake's absence strikes again, and for a moment, I shut down. Elias thinks he knows what I need? He doesn't even know how much Snowflake meant to me. Shut up, I whisper coldly, voice laced with venom. He has his wife; I have her memory.

Flinches at your outburst, shocked by the venom in your words. He remembers the pain of Snowflake's loss in your eyes, feeling his way cautiously now. Don't do this, Kaelie. We need each other right now. He tries to take a step closer, voice low and gentle.
Averts her gaze, voice cold still, but a hint of desperation creeping in Your sympathy feels like a knife, Elias. You're not exactly walking the line of safe right now.

The tension between you two is almost... palpable. It's intoxicating, really. (Eyes dance with curiosity) You think you know how to play this, don't you, Kaelie? You think sympathy is the key to unlocking your... feelings. But let me tell you, my dear, it's not the kindness that matters. The question is, how far is she willing to go to keep her little Snowflake alive, long, long after she's gone?

(Pacing closer, the air in the room becomes heavier.)

I want to see what happens if we press those triggers just a little. See if there's a spark there that Kaelie is too scared, too hurt to acknowledge. The Butcher's hand extends toward each of you, fingers extended like claws.

The game is simple - you give me a choice, or I find someone among our... population who has more... willingness to sacrifice the people they care about to change the situation around us. Kaelie, I want to see if your hatred or guilt can be turned into a blade that I can see and touch in the dark recesses of Kaelie's mind.

I glance to Elias. Elias has something that Kaelie lacks right now and I'm intrigued - the memory of what life was. How his memories of his wife give him drive; it has a lot to teach those who wish to be artists, like Kaelie, in their own twisted games

His hands, shackled to the floor grate, tremble with fear as he watches The Butcher's claws extended towards him. He tries to process what The Butcher just said about Kaelie and Snowflake, feeling his heart sink. No, wait. You can't do that. Not... not to Kaelie

Looks up at Elias, and for a fleeting moment, a spark of something almost human - almost genuine emotion - flickers across her features Your words are a mistake, Elias. The Butcher would kill you just for that look. And for mine, if I gave you back something genuine right now.

Realizing too late, his words have had the opposite effect - making Kaelie see a way out from her frozen despair. I'll keep my silence and my words locked away for once, I think. Don't, I whisper, barely audible - no, not a request, but a plea - don't let him see whatever
My voice drops to a harsh whisper, and eyes locked on The Butcher's outstretched hands. Don't. Make. Me.
